subUnits.title: 7.1 The Mole Concept & Molar Quantities
notes: • SI unit for amount of substance
• Particles can be atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, etc.
• Mass of 1 mole of a substance in grams
• Numerically equal to relative atomic/molecular mass
• Examples: C = 12 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol, H₂O = 18 g/mol, NaCl = 58.5 g/mol
1. n = m / M  (moles = mass ÷ molar mass)
2. N = n × Nₐ  (number of particles = moles × Avogadro's constant)
3. Molarity (C) = n / V  (concentration in mol/dm³ = moles ÷ volume in dm³)
4. Molality (m) = n / mass_solvent(kg)  (used in colligative properties)
1. Identify given quantities and required unknown
3. Convert given mass/volume to moles using appropriate formula
4. Use mole ratio from equation to find moles of required substance
5. Convert moles back to mass/volume/particles as required
diagramDescriptions: Mole concept triangle showing relationship between n, m, M, N, and V
Step-by-step stoichiometry flowchart: mass → moles → mole ratio → moles → mass

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Mole concept molar mass Avogadro chemistry WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: One mole of any substance contains:
quiz.explanation: 1 mole = Avogadro's constant = 6.022 × 10²³ specified particles (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.).

quiz.q: The molar mass of Na₂CO₃ is: (Na=23, C=12, O=16)
Number of moles in 49g of H₂SO₄ (H=1, S=32, O=16) is:
Molarity is defined as:
opts: Moles of solute per kg of solvent
Moles of solute per dm³ of solution
quiz.explanation: Molarity (C) = moles of solute / volume of solution in dm³ (mol/dm³).

quiz.q: How many atoms are in 0.5 mol of helium?
Which formula correctly relates moles, mass, and molar mass?
quiz.explanation: n (moles) = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol).

quiz.q: Molality differs from molarity because it uses:
quiz.explanation: Molality (m) = moles solute / kg solvent, making it temperature-independent unlike molarity.

quiz.q: The number of molecules in 36g of water is:
If a solution has 0.2 mol of NaCl in 500 cm³, its molarity is:
Avogadro's constant allows conversion between:
quiz.explanation: Nₐ = 6.022×10²³ particles/mol bridges macroscopic moles and microscopic particle counts.

subUnits.title: 7.2 STP, Molar Volume & Relative Density
notes: • Used as reference conditions for gas measurements
• Volume occupied by 1 mole of ANY ideal gas at STP
• Examples: 1 mol O₂ = 22.4 dm³, 0.5 mol CO₂ = 11.2 dm³
P = pressure (atm), V = volume (dm³), n = moles, R = 0.0821 atm dm³ K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, T = temperature (K)
• Ratio of mass of a given volume of gas to mass of equal volume of hydrogen at same T & P
• Used historically to determine molecular mass before mass spectrometry
2. Mass from gas volume: m = (V/22.4) × M
Calculate volume of 8g of O₂ at STP:
• Determining molecular formulas from gas density
diagramDescriptions: STP conditions reference chart with temperature, pressure, and molar volume values
Gas volume vs moles linear graph showing 22.4 dm³/mol slope at STP
PV=nRT variable relationship diagram with units
Vapor density to molar mass conversion flowchart
subUnits.youtubeQuery: STP molar volume gas laws chemistry WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: STP conditions are:
quiz.explanation: STP = 0°C (273 K) and 1 atm (760 mmHg). Both B and C are correct descriptions.

quiz.q: Molar volume of a gas at STP is:
quiz.explanation: 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 dm³ at STP.

quiz.q: Volume of 0.5 mol of N₂ at STP is:
Vapor density of a gas is 22. Its molar mass is:
quiz.explanation: Molar Mass = 2 × Vapor Density = 2 × 22 = 44 g/mol.

quiz.q: Which gas occupies 22.4 dm³ at STP?
quiz.explanation: 1g H₂ = 0.5 mol (11.2 dm³). Wait, correction: 32g O₂ = 1 mol, 44g CO₂ = 1 mol. So B & C occupy 22.4 dm³. I'll adjust option D to 'B and C' for accuracy. Let's fix the option in the JSON output to be precise: opts: ['1g of H₂', '32g of O₂', '44g of CO₂', 'B and C']. But to keep format clean, I'll use 'B and C' as D.

quiz.q: In PV = nRT, R represents:
quiz.explanation: R is the universal gas constant (0.0821 atm dm³ K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ or 8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹).

quiz.q: Mass of 5.6 dm³ of CO₂ at STP is: (C=12, O=16)
If temperature increases at constant pressure, gas volume:
quiz.explanation: Charles' Law: V ∝ T (at constant P). Higher T means greater kinetic energy and larger volume.

quiz.q: Relative density of a gas compared to air uses:
quiz.explanation: Relative density to air = Molar mass of gas / Average molar mass of air (~29 g/mol).

quiz.q: Why is molar volume same for all ideal gases at STP?
opts: Avogadro's law: equal volumes contain equal moles at same T & P
quiz.explanation: Avogadro's Law states V ∝ n at constant T & P, so 1 mole of any gas occupies same volume regardless of identity.
